abstract: Electrochemical propylene epoxidation using water-derived intermediates offers a sustainable path toward propylene oxide production, avoiding the hazardous chlorine or peroxide reagents typical of traditional methods. We report an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst (PdPtO$_x$/C) that achieves a Faradaic efficiency of 66 ± 5% for propylene epoxidation at 50 milliamperes per square centimeter under ambient conditions. By integrating platinum into the palladium oxide crystal lattice, we stabilized high-valence platinum species, which significantly enhanced catalytic activity and selectivity. Kinetic analysis indicates that the reaction follows a mechanism of electrophilic attack by metal-bound peroxo (OO*) intermediates. This discovery provides a versatile strategy for the selective transfer of oxygen atoms from water without the need for chemical mediators, opening new possibilities for various sustainable oxygenation reactions.
